preFix.title: Leek and potato soup
preFix.subtitle: Silky, mild, and deeply satisfying
preFix.excerpt: A pale green soup built on slowly sweated leeks and floury potatoes, blended until smooth and finished with a swirl of cream. Ready in 40 minutes and freezes well.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Potato and leek soup entry, basic white soup method.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. White soup and vegetable puree techniques.

content.text: Leek and potato soup is one of the simplest soups there is, and one of the most satisfying. The leeks give it a mild sweetness and a pale celadon colour; the potatoes give body and a silky texture when blended. It comes together in about 40 minutes and tastes better the next day.
What you need

items.name: Leek
items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, washed and sliced

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper, peeled and cut into 2 cm chunks

content.text: A deep casserole or saucepan works well here. You need the stick blender to reach comfortably to the bottom, so a wide, tall pot is better than a shallow frying pan.
Method
Sweat the alliums
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large casserole over a low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and a good pinch of salt.
Sweat
for 8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft and translucent.
Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and {{leek}} leeks. Cook for a further 5 minutes, stirring, until the leeks collapse and soften.
Add the potatoes and stock
Tip in the {{potato}} of potato chunks and pour over {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook over a medium-low heat for 20 minutes, until the potatoes are completely tender when pressed with a spoon.
Blend and finish
Remove from the heat. Use a stick blender to
purée
until completely smooth. If using a jug blender, let the soup cool slightly first and work in batches.
Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream. Taste and adjust salt. Reheat gently without boiling.
Ladle into bowls. Add a thin swirl of cream and a scatter of snipped chives.

items.fix: Use Maris Piper, King Edward, or another floury variety. Waxy potatoes (Charlotte, Jersey Royal) hold their shape rather than breaking down into a creamy purée.
items.cause: The potato variety was waxy rather than floury, giving it less starch to thicken the blend.

items.fix: Add hot stock or water, 100 ml at a time, until it reaches the consistency you want. Taste and re-season after each addition.
items.cause: The potatoes absorbed more liquid than expected, or the stock reduced during cooking.
items.symptom: Soup is too thick after blending

items.fix: Add salt gradually, tasting as you go. A small squeeze of lemon juice lifts it. If the stock was weak, a teaspoon of white wine vinegar has the same effect.
items.cause: Underseasoned at the start, or the stock was weak.

items.fix: Use only the white and pale green parts. Make sure the leeks are completely tender before blending.
items.cause: The dark green tops were included, or the leeks were not cooked long enough before blending.
items.symptom: Leeks are stringy in the final soup

content.text: Variations

For vichyssoise, chill the finished soup and serve cold with extra cream and chives. For a lighter version, swap the cream for a splash of whole milk. Adding a knob of butter stirred in at the end just before serving gives a glossy, richer result.
Make ahead and freezing

The soup keeps in the fridge for three days in a sealed container. Freeze before adding the cream: pour into bags or boxes, label, and freeze for up to three months. Defrost overnight in the fridge or from frozen in a saucepan over low heat, then add cream once hot.
Where this dish lives

Leek and potato soup is the quiet workhorse of British home cooking. British cookbooks from the 1860s onward describe the same basic formula: leeks, potatoes, stock, and cream. It appears on greasy-spoon menus and in Michelin kitchens alike. Vichyssoise, the chilled French version, was invented in New York in the early twentieth century but borrowed its ingredients directly from the British original.
